meta.uncertainty_note: Sample is strong enough for specific guidance, but no single hard technical keyword clears 60%, so do not overfit the CV around one tool or cloud badge alone.

category_signature.one_sentence_summary: A top-third CV for this category should present a senior hands-on architect who designs and ships production AI platforms and enterprise integrations, translates architecture into delivery, and proves governance, scale, and cross-functional execution without inflating people-management scope.
distinctive_signals: Architecture-first category: platform design appears in 87/88 roles (98.9%), with greenfield plus optimization work more common than pure greenfield (both 42% vs greenfield 7% vs optimization 6%).
Role shape is senior IC with some player-coach behavior, not people management: senior_ic 79%, ic 49%, player_coach 38%, manager 1%; hiring and performance management each 2/88 (2.3%).

AI architecture emphasis is production-oriented and enterprise-facing: agents/orchestration 34/88 (38.6%), guardrails/governance 33/88 (37.5%), RAG 25/88 (28.4%), evaluation/quality 18/88 (20.5%), consulting/consultancy signals 20 roles combined.

headline_pattern.recommended_structure: <Safe architect title family> | Enterprise AI / Solution Architecture | Production GenAI, Agents, Integration, Governance
evidence_first_rules: Use a title family supported by actual prior role scope; solutions_architect titles dominate the sample (50%) and ai_architect titles are also common (37%).
Pair the title with 2-4 proven architecture keywords, not a long tool list; no hard skill exceeds 60%, so the headline should not hinge on one framework or cloud.

Only use customer-facing or solution-architecture wording if the CV shows client or stakeholder interaction; stakeholder management appears in 39/88 roles (44.3%), but deep-analysis flags customer-facing claims as unsafe if not evidenced.
Do not imply people leadership in the headline unless explicit; hiring and performance management are each only 2/88 (2.3%).
citations: hard_skills_top: python 29/88 (33.0%) is highest; no hard skill >= 60%

positioning_angle: Position as a hands-on AI architecture lead for enterprise systems: show platform design, system integration, production rollout, and governance rather than pure model advanced experimentation.
Position as a translator between business/stakeholders and engineering delivery when true: communication 67/88 (76.1%), collaboration 56/88 (63.6%), stakeholder management 39/88 (44.3%).
foreground: Show architecture decisions, delivery outcomes, scale, reliability, evaluation, governance, and cost-aware productionization; these evidence types dominate deep analysis: architecture 20, delivery 19, scale 15, reliability 11, evaluation 11, governance 10, cost 7.
avoid: Avoid presenting as a pure people manager, hiring leader, or org builder; org_building is 10/88 (11.4%) and direct management signals are minimal.
Avoid advanced experimentation/formal external credential framing; requires_advanced academic is 0/88 (0.0%) and advanced experimentation_heavy is 0/88 (0.0%).
Avoid generic 'LLM expert' copy without enterprise integration proof, architecture proof, and delivery metrics.
safe_positioning: Safe: 'Senior AI architect delivering production RAG/agent systems, enterprise integrations, and guardrailed rollout' when those elements are evidenced.
Safe: 'Hands-on solution architect for AI-enabled platforms across cloud, APIs, and distributed systems' when cloud and integration work are shown.
Safe: 'Player-coach technical lead' only if mentoring, design reviews, or implementation guidance are evidenced; mentoring appears in 18/88 (20.5%), while formal management is weak.
unsafe_positioning: Unsafe without evidence: customer-facing solution architecture, full-stack delivery, DevOps/infrastructure ownership, AWS ownership, public-sector specialization, German C1, DSGVO/EU AI Act program ownership.
Unsafe without evidence: executive ownership of strategy, budget, P&L, or org design; budget_pnl is 0/88 (0.0%).
citations: valued_evidence_types: architecture 20, delivery 19, scale 15, reliability 11, evaluation 11, governance 10, cost 7

themes.theme: Enterprise AI platform and solution architecture
themes.why_it_matters: This category is architecture-led; show target architectures, system boundaries, platform patterns, and how AI components fit into enterprise stacks. Prove both new design and improvement work, not just prototypes.
themes.ats_guidance: Strongest keyword. Mention 'AI architecture' or 'solution architecture' 2-3 times across headline, profile, and experience if true. Pair once with 'platform design' and once with 'enterprise architecture' or 'target architecture' if true.
themes.citation: platform_design 87/88 (98.9%); solution architecture 11/88 (12.5%); valued evidence: architecture 20, delivery 19

themes.theme: Distributed integration across APIs, microservices, and data flows
themes.why_it_matters: The market expects architects who connect AI into real systems, especially APIs, microservices, and data pipelines rather than isolated notebooks.
themes.ats_guidance: Secondary keyword cluster. Mention 1-2 times each for 'APIs', 'microservices', and 'data pipelines' if true. Use niche integration terms once only.
themes.citation: apis 10/88 (11.4%); microservices 12/88 (13.6%); data pipelines 15/88 (17.0%); pain points include Java/Spring microservice integration (2 mentions)

themes.theme: Cloud architecture with flexible provider framing
themes.why_it_matters: Cloud is important, but the evidence does not support over-committing to one provider unless explicitly evidenced; multi-cloud appears more often than a single named cloud.
themes.ats_guidance: Use the actual provider once in headline/profile and 1-2 times in experience only if true. Prefer 'cloud architecture' or 'multi-cloud' over forced AWS/Azure repetition when experience spans both.
themes.citation: cloud_preference: multi_cloud 29%, Azure 15%, AWS 11%, GCP 1%; aws 14/88 (15.9%); azure 14/88 (15.9%)

themes.why_it_matters: Agentic delivery is a distinctive signal here and appears more often than many individual tools. Show orchestration patterns, external system connections, and production controls.
themes.ats_guidance: Strongest keyword. Mention 'agents' or 'agent orchestration' 2-3 times if true across profile and impact bullets. Name MCP or orchestration frameworks once only if explicitly evidenced.
themes.citation: agents_orchestration 34/88 (38.6%); pain points include secure scalable AI agents connecting to external systems through MCP (2 mentions)

themes.theme: RAG, vector search, and knowledge-grounded workflows
themes.why_it_matters: RAG-style architectures are common enough to materially help positioning, especially when paired with enterprise data integration and retrieval quality.
themes.ats_guidance: Strong secondary cluster. Mention 'RAG' 1-2 times and 'vector databases' or 'vector search' 1-2 times if true. Name a framework such as LangChain or LlamaIndex once only if true.

themes.why_it_matters: This category values production quality and governance, not just feature shipping. Show evaluation design, risk controls, and measurable quality or safety improvements.
themes.ats_guidance: Strongest keyword cluster. Mention 'guardrails', 'evaluation', or 'quality' 2-3 times across profile and experience if true; keep vendor observability tools to once only.
themes.citation: guardrails_governance 33/88 (37.5%); evaluation_quality 18/88 (20.5%); valued evidence: evaluation 11, governance 10, reliability 11

themes.why_it_matters: Deep analysis prioritizes delivery and reliability evidence. CV bullets should show shipped systems, hardening work, and production outcomes rather than design-only influence.
themes.ats_guidance: Use 'production', 'reliability', or 'rollout' 2-3 times across recent experience if true. Quantify uptime, latency, incident reduction, or release stability where possible.
themes.citation: valued evidence: delivery 19, reliability 11; success metrics include performance 5, security 3, production-grade delivery 2, reliability 2

themes.theme: MLOps, CI/CD, Kubernetes, and deployment discipline
themes.why_it_matters: These are recurring operational signals, but none are universal. They strengthen credibility when the candidate truly owned deployment or lifecycle work.
themes.ats_guidance: Secondary keyword cluster. Mention each of 'MLOps', 'CI/CD', and 'Kubernetes' 1-2 times if true; do not claim platform ownership if the CV only shows application-layer use.
themes.citation: mlops 17/88 (19.3%); kubernetes 16/88 (18.2%); ci/cd 11/88 (12.5%); deep-analysis flags DevOps/infrastructure ownership as unsafe if unsupported

themes.why_it_matters: Architect hires are rewarded for making systems tractable at enterprise scale, reducing risk, and modernizing platforms, not just adding AI features.
themes.ats_guidance: Use quantified outcomes in experience bullets. Mention 'scale', 'performance', 'cost', or 'modernization' 1-2 times where backed by numbers.
themes.citation: valued evidence: scale 15, cost 7; success metrics include performance 5, scalability 3, technical debt 2, architectural risks 2, modernization opportunities 2

themes.why_it_matters: This category strongly rewards architects who can align technical decisions with delivery stakeholders and adjacent teams.
themes.ats_guidance: Mention 'communication' or 'cross-functional collaboration' 2-3 times when true, especially in profile and recent roles. Keep it tied to concrete architecture or delivery outcomes.

themes.why_it_matters: Stakeholder translation matters, but the sample does not support broad executive or business-ownership claims by default. Keep scope precise.
themes.ats_guidance: Mention 'stakeholder management' 1-2 times if true. Use exact stakeholder groups once only, such as product, security, data, or client teams.

themes.theme: Mentoring and design guidance as limited player-coach evidence
themes.why_it_matters: There is some player-coach behavior, but formal people management is rare. Mentoring can help differentiate if the CV clearly supports it.
themes.ats_guidance: Mention 'mentoring' or 'technical guidance' once or twice only if true. Do not repeat manager-language across the CV.

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: End-to-end enterprise AI architecture delivered into production
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Proves the core category fit: architecture ownership plus delivery, not concept work alone.
pain_points_addressed: need to solve complex enterprise architecture problems end-to-end
integrate ai and data components into existing enterprise systems
guide implementation from target architecture to production rollout
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Use ARIS: Architecture challenge, design decisions, implementation path, shipped outcome. Make the design choice explicit, then quantify the production result.
key_achievement_archetypes.citation: platform_design 87/88 (98.9%); valued evidence: architecture 20, delivery 19; success metrics include performance 5 and production-grade delivery 2

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: Agentic or RAG system with governance and evaluation controls
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Shows modern GenAI architecture depth with the production safeguards this category values.
pain_points_addressed: build secure, scalable AI agents connected to external systems
apply strong security to agent and MCP integrations
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Use STAR with risk emphasis: situation and trust constraints, technical approach for agents/RAG/guardrails, measured quality or safety outcome, and deployment scope.

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: Complex integration into APIs, microservices, and enterprise data estates
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Demonstrates the category's difference from generic GenAI roles: integration architecture inside real enterprise systems.
pain_points_addressed: integrate ai and data components into java/spring-based microservices
optimize relational and nosql data access and caching for performance
connect AI services to existing APIs and platform boundaries
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Use STAR. Emphasize the legacy or distributed constraint, the integration pattern chosen, and the measurable operational improvement.
key_achievement_archetypes.citation: microservices 12/88 (13.6%); apis 10/88 (11.4%); data pipelines 15/88 (17.0%); pain points include Java/Spring integration and data access optimization (2 mentions each)

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: Cloud modernization or multi-cloud architecture improvement
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Shows ability to improve existing environments, reduce risk, and scale AI-enabled platforms pragmatically.
pain_points_addressed: need to scale cloud platforms for large enterprise environments
need to improve existing architectures and algorithms
reduce architectural risk and technical debt while adding AI capability
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Use ARIS. Start with platform constraints or debt, show the target-state architecture, then quantify operational or cost impact.
key_achievement_archetypes.citation: cloud_preference: multi_cloud 29%, Azure 15%, AWS 11%; valued evidence: scale 15, cost 7; success metrics include scalability 3, technical debt 2, architectural risks 2, modernization opportunities 2

key_achievement_archetypes.archetype: Technical guidance and cross-functional architecture translation
key_achievement_archetypes.what_it_proves: Adds player-coach credibility without overstating formal management scope.
pain_points_addressed: guide team members through AI integration patterns and code quality
align engineering, security, product, and stakeholders around architecture decisions
turn architecture into delivery standards and implementation guidance
key_achievement_archetypes.story_format_guidance: Use STAR. Focus on the technical leadership mechanism such as standards, reviews, or mentoring, then quantify delivery or quality impact rather than team-size prestige.

highest_weight_roles: Roles with explicit architecture ownership, especially AI Architect, AI Solution Architect, Solutions Architect, or equivalent senior technical architect roles.
Roles showing end-to-end delivery of AI-enabled platforms, enterprise integrations, distributed systems, or cloud modernization.
Hands-on technical lead roles should be weighted highly when they show architecture decisions plus delivery outcomes, even if the title is not pure 'architect'.
expand_in_work_history: Expand roles with architecture proof, production delivery, stakeholder translation, platform design, cloud/system integration, evaluation, governance, or reliability outcomes.
Expand work that shows enterprise consulting or consultancy context when true, because consulting/consultancy signals recur in 20 roles combined and solution-architecture titles dominate.
Expand roles where non-AI systems experience maps cleanly to distributed systems, APIs, microservices, data platforms, or resilience patterns, then bridge that work to later AI architecture.
compress_in_work_history: Compress roles that are mostly people management, generic delivery management, advanced experimentation, or isolated model experimentation without production architecture.
Compress shallow tool-only roles that cannot show ownership of architecture, integration, or measurable deployment outcomes.
Compress early roles with limited relevance once 2-4 strong architecture roles are established.
how_to_frame_non_ai_experience: Frame prior backend, platform, cloud, distributed systems, or data engineering work as the foundation for current AI architecture; emphasize service boundaries, resilience, performance, integration, and modernization.
Translate non-AI architecture experience into adjacent evidence such as APIs, microservices, CI/CD, Kubernetes, MLOps-like operational discipline, or enterprise target-state design.
Do not force non-AI roles into GenAI language; instead show continuity from enterprise systems architecture to production AI rollout.

citations: management signals weak: hiring 2/88 (2.3%), performance_management 2/88 (2.3%), budget_pnl 0/88 (0.0%)

avoid_claims: Customer-facing solution architecture unless client interaction is explicit on the CV.
AWS or Azure architecture ownership unless the provider is named explicitly in prior work.
DevOps, infrastructure, or platform ownership unless supported by concrete deployment responsibility.
Full-stack delivery if the CV mainly shows backend, platform, or architecture work.
Direct public-sector delivery, smart-city, or government deployment claims unless evidenced.
German C1, Polish, or other native-language claims unless explicitly shown.
Hands-on ownership of GDPR, DSGVO, EU AI Act, or responsible-AI programs unless the CV proves delivery scope.
Formal people management, hiring, org building, budget, or direct-report ownership unless explicitly evidenced.
buzzword_patterns: Long undifferentiated tool lists without architecture outcomes.
Generic 'LLM expert' or 'GenAI specialist' wording with no production, governance, or integration proof.
Leadership-heavy language that does not show mentoring, stakeholder translation, or architecture decisions.
Cloud badge stacking when multi-cloud or provider-neutral architecture is the safer truth.
title_inflation_risks: Inflating technical lead or senior engineer roles into director or head titles.
Using 'Chief' or 'VP' language in a category where senior IC and player-coach signals dominate.
Using 'manager' framing by default when manager roles are only 1% and senior_manager roles 3%.
research_framing_risks: Do not add research, publication, or PhD framing as a positive signal in this category.
Do not describe experimentation alone as category fit; convert it into architecture, delivery, evaluation, and governance evidence or omit it.
domain_or_region_risks: Do not imply consulting, enterprise SaaS, or public-sector depth unless the CV shows those sectors explicitly.
Do not imply remote-global breadth or multilingual fluency beyond what the CV supports.
Do not imply region-specific compliance leadership from isolated exposure.
citations: deep_analysis unsafe_claims_top includes customer-facing, AWS ownership, DevOps ownership, full-stack, public-sector, German C1, DSGVO/EU AI Act ownership

evidence_ledger.recommendation: Anchor the CV around hands-on architecture and production delivery rather than executive leadership.
Treat communication, collaboration, and stakeholder translation as core signals, but keep management claims narrow.
Use agentic systems, guardrails, governance, and RAG as differentiators when explicitly evidenced.
Do not overfit the CV around one tool, cloud, or language because the technical stack is fragmented.
support: highest hard skill is python 29/88 (33.0%); programming_languages required python 31/88 (35.2%)

evidence_ledger.recommendation: Weight enterprise integration and modernization stories heavily; this category differs from adjacent AI roles by expecting AI to fit existing systems and architecture constraints.
support: pain points include Java/Spring integration, data access optimization, enterprise architecture problems, and cloud scaling

evidence_ledger.recommendation: Keep research, publication, PhD, and formal people-management framing inside risk controls rather than positive positioning.
Use ATS repetition selectively: strongest 4-6 keywords should be architecture, production delivery, agents, guardrails/governance, RAG, and enterprise integration when true.
support: communication 67/88 (76.1%) and collaboration 56/88 (63.6%) support integration/stakeholder wording

evidence_ledger.recommendation: Use cloud-provider keywords conservatively; prefer provider-neutral architecture unless the CV has named delivery on that platform.
support: cloud_preference: multi_cloud 29% exceeds Azure 15% and AWS 11%
deep-analysis unsafe claims flag AWS ownership if not shown explicitly

evidence_ledger.recommendation: Mentoring can be presented as limited player-coach evidence, but not as broad team management.
This category is distinct from research-heavy AI roles because hiring evidence rewards shipped architecture, reliability, evaluation, governance, and integration over model novelty.
support: valued evidence: architecture 20, delivery 19, reliability 11, evaluation 11, governance 10
